subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Clear and logical overall structure with recognizable introduction, body and conclusion.
Effective use of headings, numbered figures and tables to guide the reader.
Coherent development of the argument through sections (aim â data â mathematics â model â conclusion).
weaknesses: Occasional rambling passages and digressions reduce conciseness.
Repetition of the aim and verbose narrative in places.
Inconsistent formatting of raw tables (e.g. Figure 12) affects clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and well-organized, meeting the âwell organized with clear introduction, body and conclusionâ descriptor (band 3). However, conciseness suffers from verbose passages and some formatting inconsistencies, so it does not reach the highest level. A score of 3/4 appropriately reflects these strengths and weaknesses.

strengths: Correct presentation of the Poisson formula using Î» and factorial notation.
Basic spreadsheet and table representations support the argument at key points.
weaknesses: Inconsistent use of mathematical notation (mixing percentages and probabilities).
Limited variety of representations â missing graphs or more sophisticated visuals.
Frequent typographical errors and deeply nested IF formulas hinder clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student uses standard symbols correctly in places, the notational inconsistencies and limited, sometimes unclear representations place this work at the lower end of band 2. Given the recurrent communication errors, a 1/4 reflects that some mathematical language is present but poorly sustained.

strengths: Clear personal motivation from a genuine interest in football.
Decision to collect bespoke data and adapt Î» values shows independent thinking.
Designing and running a Monte-Carlo style simulation demonstrates initiative.
weaknesses: The personal narrative, while present, could be more tightly woven into the mathematical discussion.
Some choices (e.g. trial number, Î» adjustments) lack commentary on alternative approaches.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is significant evidence of independent thinking and a clear personal approach driving the investigation, matching band 2. Creativity is evident but not outstanding enough for the top band, so 2/3 is justified.

strengths: Reflection on sampling bias and model limitations shows awareness.
Comments link the outcomes to understanding of Poisson assumptions.
weaknesses: Depth of reflection remains surface-level without thorough quantification of biases.
Critical analysis lacks statistical rigour and is not sustained throughout.
Connections to broader implications and validation are suggested but unfulfilled.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student offers meaningful reflections and some critique (band 2) but does not develop them into substantial critical analysis or thread them consistently. A 1/3 reflects limited depth of reflection overall.

strengths: Appropriate application of Poisson distribution and expected value calculations within SL syllabus.
Correct discussion of independence and discrete-event assumptions.
Basic Monte-Carlo simulation logic is logically applied.
weaknesses: Rounding inconsistencies and percentage/probability confusions introduce substantive errors.
Some computations (e.g. Î» adjustments, impact calculations) lack full justification.
Missing summary statistics to highlight key trends from raw data.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematics lies solidly at course level (band 3) but is only partially correct in places, with substantive errors and gaps in justification. A score of 2/6 reflects relevant use of mathematics with noticeable inaccuracies and limited rigour.

strandSelections.reasoning: Choosing a personal data set, calculating bespoke Î» values and building a Monte-Carlo style model show genuine independent decision-making beyond textbook examples, satisfying âsignificant evidenceâ.

Explanations of the Poisson model, expected value and simulation logic show reasonable grasp, though deeper theoretical justification is absent.  Understanding is therefore âgood in partsâ rather than thorough.

Key calculations (e.g. Poisson probabilities) are mostly correct, but percentage/probability confusion (0.145%), rounding inconsistencies and mis-copied Î» adjustments introduce substantive errors.  Hence accuracy is only âpartially correctâ.

Most material is relevant, but large blocks of raw penalty tables and verbose narrative add length without mathematical benefit, and some repetition (e.g. restating aim) detracts from conciseness.  Hence the work is âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ.

Standard symbols (Î», P(x)) and factorial notation are used correctly in places, yet the student mixes percentages and probabilities (0.145%) and occasionally mis-formats subscripts.  Appropriate language appears, but not consistently enough for a higher band.

Explanations are usually understandable, but occasional inconsistencies (e.g. swapping between decimal and percentage) and typographical errors reduce clarity.  Overall, communication is âmostly clear with some consistencyâ.

The Poisson distribution, expected value calculations and a basic Monte-Carlo simulation are solidly within SL course content and are applied appropriately to the context.  No work beyond syllabus level is attempted, so the ârelevant mathematics at course level usedâ band fits.

Tables, a small spreadsheet formula image and a single probability table constitute âsome appropriate representationsâ.  Missing graphs/diagrams prevent more sophisticated multi-representation use.

Reflection appears in both conclusion and evaluation, recognising sampling bias, small Î», and model limitations.  These comments are meaningful but not deeply critical or threaded âthroughoutâ, earning the mid band.
Creating a spreadsheet-based simulation, deriving conditional adjustments and running 50 trials demonstrate noticeable creativity and initiative, though the mathematics itself remains conventional.

The exploration follows a recognisable path: introduction â aim â data â mathematics â model â conclusion/evaluation.  Despite some rambling passages and the occasional digression, each section generally leads into the next and the reader can follow the argument without major gaps.  Logical progression is therefore clear, but the quality is not refined enough for the âexcellentâ descriptor.

The student critiques unexpected findings (away penalties higher than home) and the Poisson assumptions, showing some analytical thought, yet the analysis remains surface-level without rigorous statistical testing.
Reflections explicitly link the investigation to the studentâs understanding of modelling and Monte-Carlo methods, demonstrating a clear connection, but not enough depth for the highest band.

Headings, numbered figures and tables produce a clear introduction-body-conclusion structure.  Cross-referencing is imperfect (e.g. Figure numbers sometimes mismatched) yet the overall layout is easy to navigate.  Transitions are present, so the organisation meets the âwell-organisedâ standard.
The exploration is framed around the studentâs own interest in football; this personal context steers the investigative choices throughout, amounting to a clear personal approach.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated and sets a focused investigative framework, guiding the exploration of Poisson modelling in football penalties.
The introduction demonstrates outstanding personal engagement by linking the studentâs longâterm interest in football to the mathematical investigation.
The narrative in the introduction is verbose and sometimes repetitive. Consider tightening sentences and avoiding restating the aim to improve conciseness and relevance.
The aim section succinctly outlines objectives for applying Poisson distribution and building a model, providing clear direction for the investigation.
The raw data table is extensive but lacks summary statistics. Add a concise summary (e.g., totals and proportions) to highlight key trends and improve relevance.
The discussion of Poisson assumptions effectively conveys concepts of independence and discrete events, demonstrating good understanding of underlying theory.
The Poisson formula is presented accurately and formatted clearly, supporting correct mathematical communication.
The interpretation â0.145% chanceâ miscues percentage and probability. Clarify that Î»=0.145 corresponds to a 14.5% probability, not 0.145%.
The calculation of Î» is correct but percentages are mixed with probabilities. Standardize notation: report Î»=94/649â0.145 (14.5%).
The empty probability table in Figure 4 lacks content. Complete the table with computed probabilities to make the representation effective.
Figure 5âs table of Î» values per condition mixes event rates and proportions without clear units. Clarify whether these Î» are perâgame rates or proportions of success.
The formula for expected goals is introduced correctly, but lacks connection to the Poisson model derivation. Include a rationale linking EV=NÂ·Î»Â·p to Poisson expectations.
The computation of average games per season divides total games by three players and seasons without clear justification. Explain why this approach approximates N.
Figure 6âs working column is cluttered with multiple lines. Separate the working out from the final expected values to improve clarity.
The Excel IF formula for simulating penalty counts is functional but deeply nested and reduces readability. Consider using a lookup table or SWITCH for clarity.
Impact calculations in Figure 9 treat percentage changes additively. In probabilistic models, apply multiplicative adjustments to preserve probability bounds.
The long nested IF chain in your code snippet compromises mathematical communication. Suggest presenting a structured decision table instead.
The simulation summary lacks comparison with realâworld penalty frequencies. Include validation by comparing simulated averages with actual season data.
The raw simulation results table (Figure 12) includes atypical symbols and inconsistent formatting. Simplify entries to standard numerals for clarity.
The conclusion recognizes achievement of aims but lacks critical analysis of result deviations. Reflect on discrepancies between expected and simulated values.
The reflection on convenience sampling and player selection bias is meaningful and shows awareness of methodological limitations.
While various limitations are listed, the evaluation lacks quantification of their impact. Consider estimating how each bias could quantitatively alter your results.
